But since we're tracking publicly available data, we only can account for what's available on the property website.
So this typically happens in a lease up.
But let's say that a property has, you know, 100 1 bedrooms available in it.
They decide to only make 30 available on the property website.
Our system can only account for the 30 that are made available.
We technically have no clue that the other 70 exist.
Therefore, we can't report on them.
So, advertised occupancy is based off of the known number of vacant units in the property.
So we take the vacant units divided by the total number of units.
That's how we get the advertised occupancy.

Um And in regards to that, what I always say is, you know, you know, right now, the way a renter is looking for an apartment is, you know, at night, they pretty much sit on their couch, they visit 67 different property websites.
